item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept going back and forth between an upvote (well-written, has a consistent thematic logic that marks an object that could theoretically exist in the real world, rather than being just a Narrative Thing) and a novote (the lack of an interview log or any context that might shed some light on the subject is something that makes sense as an out-of-universe but lacks any in-universe justification), but I ultimately am upvoting on the question of just who or what will emerge from the Gulf in twenty year's time.

item.title: Regarding the recent page revision
item.content:encoded: Just an FYI, I went ahead and changed the audio player from Flash to HTML5, as Flash is rapidly losing support and already can't be viewed by many people, including Firefox and Mobile users. No content changes were made, and the article is otherwise unchanged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I've misread something, but a slight time discrepancy.

"If travel along Course Panacea proceeds as expected, no further action will be required until the year 2043, approximately five years before projected landfall, at which point revised containment procedures (detailed in File 2558-F) will be implemented."

And.

At its current rate and direction (known as Course Panacea), SCP-2558 will make landfall near Panacea, Wakulla County, Florida on October 31, 2046.

Should either be 3 years before projected landfall, or 2041.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel sorry for the people though. More than usual in other skips. Upvoted either way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty great. I really wish we could have some interviews or something involving the parishioners from back when they were still able to rescue them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please give us more Dixieland Nightmare Magic, I love the depth, context and detail of these skips. There's just… it makes it more believable, feel more real-world

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'm really glad the work and thought put into the context of this object shows up. I tried to use in-universe citations to provide an immersive depth without cluttering the file (which is supposed to be a breezy, top-level overview of containment) too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was originally a somewhat hesitant downvote, but after rereading this, I've changed my mind.

The peripherals on this one are quite impressive. I normally loathe audio files in skips, as most of the time they have only the barest relevance to the rest of the narrative. In this case, it was artfully done and made sense in context. The graphics work, as with your other recent entries, is also well done. Upon first reading, I found that these elements felt overwhelming to the actual story being presented here. "Baptists being ironically punished over and over" felt thin at the time.

Upon the second reading, however, this came across like something a real-life Foundation would actually have. The real world does not offer reams and reams of convenient backstory(tm), nor does it leave helpful notes or diaries or whatever. In that light, the trappings surrounding the narrative didn't feel like a purposeful distraction from a thin narrative, but rather enhanced the sense of immersion as I read.

Overall, a solid effort. I'll support this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dixieland Nightmare Magic

The Ethics of Repeatedly Drowning Baptists

Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well. Between the story, the horror, the weirdness, the implied backstory, and the aforementioned citations, you pretty much had me at hello. +1, and sudden keen interest in reading the rest of your stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can only imagine what these people are going to be like if they actually make landfall. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. I love an article with good in-universe citations. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For recent debate of this change in policy, refer to Foundation Ethics Committee submission 1405.22 (June 8, 1998) (Anonymous, "The One Thousand Deaths of Pastor Lewis Robinson: The Ethics of Repeatedly Drowning Baptists") and Foundation Ethics Committee meeting 7561.05 (September 4, 1998).

Oh have your upvote.
In general, this is great and I love the thought put into a sensible and reasonably costed containment procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love skips that take a long time to do what they're doing. It's just trundling its way across the Gulf, not in any particular hurry, and taking everyone with it.

a pattern of anti-Protestant extranormal action

Okay, you have my attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tweaked that to be the maximum. Though, to be fair, these folks have had about a thousand weeks of practice at holding their breaths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As humans fall unconscious after about three minutes and die after approximately eight minutes in underwater situations

Is that not a long time for an average person to spend underwater before passing out? I could see a minute. Three sounds like someone trained or someone coming off a Tibetan mountain. I'd be impressed by three, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love stuff with strong implied background beyond what's in the article, and I also love articles about religious factions, especially ones as well written as this.
+1, happily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is basically some priest taking "baptism via immersion in water" WAY too seriously? If so then I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Baptism is the seal of eternal life. - St. Irenaeus

The next skip in my Dixieland Nightmare Magic series, after SCP-2072.
